burial.plot_notes: Died Kobe Japan November 1, 1911. Burial location in Japan.

parents.father_research_notes: T01063 Ralph Talcott — woolen manufacturer, co-founder of Rockville CT.
parents.mother_research_notes: I00196 Susan Bull Talcott Cotter — died 1854 when Eliza was 18.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended and later returned to teach at Miss Porter's School, Farmington CT.

narrative_blocks.body: In March 1873, thirty-seven-year-old Eliza Talcott arrived in Kobe, Japan, considered "relatively old by missionary standards." Within two years she had co-founded Kobe Girls' School with Julia Dudley, which became Kobe College in 1894. It remains open today, one hundred and fifty years later.

Talcott was dubbed the "Florence Nightingale of Japan" for her nursing work during the First Sino-Japanese War in Hiroshima in 1894. She learned Japanese quickly and was known in Japan by her transliterated name, Eliza Tarukatto. She died in Kobe on November 1, 1911, having spent nearly four decades in Japan.

narrative_blocks.header: Both parents died before she was eighteen
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph Talcott died in 1847 when Eliza was eleven; her mother Susan died in 1854 when she was eighteen. Orphaned, she was educated at the Sarah Porter School in Farmington, then returned to teach there before choosing a life of missionary service.

cross_connections.display_label: Funded Doshisha; fellow ABCFM Japan mission benefactor

burial.plot_notes: Elmwood Cemetery Vernon CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: His mill turned barren fields into a city
narrative_blocks.body: Ralph Talcott co-founded Rockville, Connecticut, where he and a partner started manufacturing cloth by machinery — the first such operation in the town. The Talcott Pedigree records: "where in 1830 there were only barren fields, there is now in 1875 a populous, picturesque and beautiful city." His daughter Eliza would go on to found a college in Japan.

burial.plot_notes: Elmwood Cemetery Vernon CT. FindAGrave confirms.

parents.father_research_notes: Susan Bull of Plymouth CT. SOURCE: Talcott book.

burial.plot_notes: Elmwood Cemetery Vernon CT. FindAGrave confirms.

narrative_blocks.header: Helped write the Connecticut Constitution of 1818
narrative_blocks.body: Deacon Phineas Talcott of Vernon served in the Connecticut General Assembly for many sessions and was one of the delegates who formed the Connecticut State Constitution of 1818 — the document that replaced the colonial charter of 1662 and separated church from state. His wife Hannah died just nineteen days after him, on January 5, 1836.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter founded a Japanese college
narrative_blocks.body: Through his son Ralph, co-founder of Rockville, Connecticut, came granddaughter Eliza Talcott — founder of Kobe College in Japan. The deacon’s line ran from Bolton’s meetinghouse to Meiji-era missionary education in two generations.

burial.plot_notes: Elmwood Cemetery Vernon CT. FindAGrave confirms. Died 19 days after her husband.

parents.father_research_notes: Daughter of Rev. Ebenezer Kellogg and Hannah Wright. SOURCE: Talcott Pedigree 1876.

INST078.name: Kobe College
INST078.notes:
Founded October 12, 1875 as the first educational institution for women in western Japan. Co-founded by T01213 Eliza Talcott and Julia Dudley, sent by the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions (ABCFM). Became Kobe College in 1894. Still operating today. Eliza Talcott is considered one of its founders. Current campus (Okadayama) built 1933, designed by architect William Merrell Vories.
[former/alt detail] Founded as Kobe Home (1873) / Kobe Girls' School (1875) / Kobe eiwa jogakko

INST067.name: Miss Porter's School
INST067.notes:
Founded 1843 by Sarah Porter (X01980) in Farmington CT. Originally operated from the stone store at 96 Main Street where John Hooker (H01626) also practiced law. First permanent schoolhouse built on site of Edward Hooker's (H00630) earlier 'Old Red College.' Known graduates include Jacqueline Kennedy Onassis, Gloria Vanderbilt, Alice Hamilton. Sarah Porter (X01980) ran the school for 57 years until her death in 1900. History of Berlin notes it was said 'a pupil of Miss Porter's school at Farmington may be known by the way she enters a room.' Connecticut Women's Hall of Fame inductee. T01213 Eliza Talcott (1836-1911) attended as a student after being orphaned; later returned to teach there before becoming a missionary to Japan.
[former/alt detail] Porter's School; Farmington School for Girls
